text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large anterior myoma on the anterior wall of the uterus, along with multiple small subserous myomas. A large pedunculated myoma is visible on the right side, attached to the posterior wall of the uterus. A uterine manipulator is introduced, and interversion is performed, exposing additional myomas on the posterior wall.
A myoma is being pulled from the right iliac fossa towards the upper abdomen, revealing the pedicle. Vasopressin is injected to induce transient ischemia in the area.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel set to minimum vibration to cut the pedicle of a pedunculated myoma attached to the posterior wall of the uterus. The myoma is slowly dissected from its attachment, and two myomas, including one large one, are removed.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device on the base of the third subserous pedunculated myoma, followed by cutting the myoma with a harmonic scalpel to detach it from the uterus.

The surgeon deposits excised myomas into the cul-de-sac, ensuring accurate counting. Vasopressin is injected into the anterior wall myoma, visibly creating a jet from puncture sites and inducing transient ischemia, making the myoma more palpable. The harmonic device is used to cut the serous layer of the anterior wall myoma.

After splitting the serous layer, the surgeon introduces a myoma screw and applies anterosuperior traction to slowly enucleate the fourth myoma. The myoma is gradually separated from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon separates the fifth pedunculated subserous myoma and places it into the cul-de-sac. The sixth myoma is visible and ready for removal.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation in the surgical field, followed by suturing the anterior wall of the myoma using a Y-Grill and a 30 mm needle. A deep bite is taken with a tumble square knot to approximate the tissue under tension.

The surgeon performs a single wrap with a suture, creating a loose square knot. The needle end is straightened in a reverse C motion, and the knot is tightened using the tip of a Maryland dissector to ensure it is secure and stops bleeding. Continuous suturing follows the initial tumble square knot.

The surgeon ties an additional lock on the temple square knot for secure closure, ensuring the knot is perfect. The assistant holds the suture as the surgeon takes further bites within the C-shaped area for interlocking. Despite being a subserous myoma, careful approximation is performed to maintain uterine wall integrity, using bidirectional barb sutures.

The surgeon takes another deep bite with the needle, using a tumble square knot to approximate the tissue with improved traction. Bleeding occurs from the needle prick due to high vascularity, but stops once the knot is tied and locked with a surgeon's knot.

The surgeon performs ipsilateral suturing with a final lock, noting the difficulty due to a manipulation angle of less than sixty degrees. Suction irrigation is used to inspect the area, revealing serosal folds. An additional suture bite is taken to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon tightens a tumbled square knot using a Maryland dissector, ensuring the knot is initially loose before sliding it to secure tissue approximation. A newly discovered myoma in the posterior wall of the uterus is separated, marking the seventh myoma identified during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully pulls out the myoma using blunt dissection, ensuring a good plane for separation. The posterior wall of the uterus is sutured, taking care to avoid damaging the fallopian tube, which is protected due to the patient's nulliparous status.
An instrument is used to lift the uterus, facilitating further suturing and knotting. The surgeon prepares to adjust the Maryland instrument, noting the projection of one hook leg.
The surgeon takes a second bite with the suture needle, forming a C and reverse C pattern. Bleeding is visible but is expected to stop immediately after the bites are secured.

The surgeon uses a guarding technique to pull the suture through the loop and locks it. The assistant lifts the uterus, allowing knots to be placed and tightened at the appropriate locations. A further lock is applied, and minor bleeding is visible, which will be managed later with fulguration, avoiding excessive use of electrocautery.

The surgeon uses a Maryland instrument to tighten the suture, preparing to cut it. Thorough suction and irrigation are performed in the uterine cavity, confirming no visible myomas remain. The suture is trimmed, and there is no active bleeding.

The surgeon performs thorough suction and irrigation of the surgical field. The final step involves careful morselation of the large myoma, with one ear of the morselator visible during the process. The myoma is slowly morselated out, emphasizing the necessity of this step in laparoscopic myomectomy for large myomas.
So thank you very much for watching this video. This was just a simple case of multiple myoma in the uterus which has been taken by laparoscopy. Thank you very much.
